content.text: Projects in Claude.ai are persistent workspaces that let you organize conversations, instructions, and background knowledge around a specific goal or context. Instead of starting every conversation from scratch with the same setup prompt, a project preserves your instructions and shared files so that every new conversation in the project begins with that context already loaded.
What Projects Are

A Claude.ai Project is a named container that holds three things: a set of project instructions (a persistent system prompt), a project knowledge base (uploaded files or text snippets that Claude can reference), and a collection of related conversations. When you start a new chat inside a project, Claude automatically has access to the project's instructions and knowledge without you needing to re-paste them.

Projects are available on Claude.ai Pro, Team, and Enterprise plans. Each project is private to the account that created it by default, though Team and Enterprise plans can share projects among workspace members. Projects persist indefinitely — they do not expire when you close the browser or start a new session.
Why Projects Matter

The biggest friction in using AI assistants for recurring tasks is context setup. If you use Claude regularly as a writing editor, you probably type the same style preferences at the start of every conversation: tone, audience, formatting rules, what to avoid. If you use Claude for code review, you probably re-explain the codebase conventions and tech stack every session. Projects eliminate this repeated setup by storing your instructions and background knowledge persistently.

Projects also improve response quality. When Claude has access to relevant context files — a style guide, a technical spec, a company glossary — from the start of every conversation, it produces responses that are more consistent and accurate to your actual requirements. Without projects, a response about your codebase requires you to first explain the codebase; with a project, Claude already knows the relevant context.

For teams, projects provide a shared context layer. All team members working in a shared project see the same instructions and knowledge, which means consistent AI behavior across the team without each member needing to maintain their own setup prompts.
How to Create a Project
Log into Claude.ai and look at the left sidebar. You should see a Projects section.
Click the + New project button (or similar; the exact label varies with UI versions).
A dialog or new project page appears asking for a project name.
Type a clear, descriptive name for the project (see naming conventions below).
Optionally add a project description explaining the project's purpose.
Click Create or Save to create the project.
You are taken to the project page, which shows tabs or sections for Instructions, Knowledge, and Conversations.
From here, add instructions and knowledge before starting your first conversation.
Project Naming Conventions
A good project name is specific enough to distinguish it from other projects but short enough to scan quickly in a sidebar. The following patterns work well:
Function + Context
— e.g. 'Code Review — Python Backend', 'Editing — Technical Blog', 'Research — Market Analysis'
Client + Purpose
— e.g. 'Acme Corp — Support', 'Project Titan — Documentation'
Tool + Domain
— e.g. 'SQL Queries — Analytics DB', 'API Design — Mobile App'

Avoid generic names like 'Claude Project 1' or 'My Project' — they give no indication of the project's purpose and make the sidebar cluttered when you have multiple projects. Also avoid overly long names; 4–6 words is usually enough.
Poor Name
Better Name
Project 1
Blog Writing — Tech Topics
Claude assistant
Code Review — Node.js API
Work stuff
Legal Research — Contract Templates
Writing
Marketing Copy — B2B SaaS
Project Settings and Configuration
Project Instructions

Instructions are the persistent system prompt for all conversations in the project. They tell Claude how to behave in this project: what role to play, what style to use, what constraints to follow, and what background knowledge to apply. Full details are covered in the Project Instructions sub-subtopic; briefly, good instructions are specific, behavioral, and include examples where relevant.
Project Knowledge

Knowledge files are documents, code snippets, or text blocks that Claude can reference in every project conversation. Upload reference materials — a style guide, an API spec, a product brief, a glossary — and Claude draws on them when generating responses. The Project Knowledge sub-subtopic covers this in detail.
Conversation Organization

All conversations started inside a project appear in the project's conversation list, grouped separately from conversations outside any project. You can rename conversations within a project for easy navigation, and you can archive or delete old conversations when they are no longer needed.
Organizing Multiple Projects
As your use of Claude grows, you may accumulate many projects. A few organizational strategies keep the project list manageable:
Organize by Function

Group projects by the type of work they support: writing projects, coding projects, research projects, client projects. Each function has its own distinct instructions and knowledge, so functional grouping maps naturally onto projects.
Organize by Client or Stakeholder

For client-facing work, one project per client ensures that client-specific instructions and context stay separate. This avoids accidental cross-contamination where client A's style preferences affect client B's deliverables.
Combine Small Related Tasks

Not every use case warrants its own project. If you have two or three small recurring tasks that share similar instructions, consolidating them into one project with slightly broader instructions is cleaner than having five projects with near-identical setups.
Archive Completed Projects

When a project is complete — the product launched, the contract closed, the research published — rename or archive it to keep the active project list short. Most interfaces support this to prevent old projects from cluttering the sidebar.
Creating Projects for Specific Use Cases
Software Development Project

Project Name: Code Review — Python API
Instructions:
You are a senior Python engineer. Review code for PEP 8 compliance,
type annotation coverage, test coverage, and security issues.
Always explain the reason for each suggestion, not just the change.
Use Python 3.12+ idioms. The project uses FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, and Pydantic.

Knowledge files to add:
- coding-standards.md
- api-spec.yaml
- database-schema.sql
Content Writing Project

Project Name: Blog Writing — DevTools Newsletter
Instructions:
You are an experienced technical writer for a developer audience.
Write in a clear, direct, slightly informal tone. Avoid marketing buzzwords.
Keep sentences under 25 words where possible. Use active voice.
Target reader: mid-level software engineer who reads during coffee breaks.

Knowledge files to add:
- style-guide.pdf
- past-issues-sample.md (3–5 example posts for tone reference)
- editorial-calendar.csv
Research Project

Project Name: Competitive Research — AI Code Tools
Instructions:
You are a research analyst. Provide objective, data-backed analysis.
Cite sources wherever possible. Note uncertainty rather than speculating.
Use tables for comparisons. Provide a confidence level for claims
where sources conflict or are limited.

Knowledge files to add:
- competitor-list.md
- current-analysis-notes.txt
What Projects Do Not Do
Understanding the boundaries of the Projects feature prevents false expectations:
Projects do not share context across conversations
— each conversation in a project starts fresh from instructions and knowledge, but does not see what was said in other project conversations.
Projects do not update automatically
— if you update the instructions or add a knowledge file, previous conversations are not retroactively affected. Only new conversations and new messages in open conversations see the updated context.
Projects do not give Claude internet access
— adding instructions telling Claude to 'search the web' does not enable web search unless the web search feature is separately activated.
Projects are not databases

— project knowledge is read-only context, not a database Claude can query with structured filters. Keep knowledge files small enough to be useful as context rather than expecting database-style retrieval.
Common Mistakes When Creating Projects
Creating Too Many Projects

A common initial impulse is to create a project for every task. This leads to dozens of thin projects, each used once or twice, that clutter the sidebar and make navigation harder. Create a project only when a recurring set of tasks shares enough common instructions and context to justify the setup effort.
Using Vague Instructions

A project with instructions that say 'Help me with writing' is only marginally more useful than a project with no instructions, because there is nothing specific for Claude to follow. Instructions should be behavioral and specific: 'When editing, focus on reducing passive voice and replacing jargon with plain English.'
Neglecting to Update Knowledge Files

Knowledge files quickly become stale if not maintained. An API spec uploaded six months ago may not reflect current endpoints. A style guide from two years ago may not include recently adopted conventions. Set a reminder to review and update project knowledge files periodically.
Best Practices
Create a project only when a use case is truly recurring
— at least 5–10 conversations that share the same instructions and context
Write specific, behavioral instructions
not vague aspirations; see the Project Instructions guide for templates
Start small with knowledge
— add only the most essential reference files and expand as needed, rather than uploading everything at once
Name projects to aid navigation
— descriptive names that tell you at a glance what the project is for
Review projects regularly
— archive or delete projects that are no longer active to keep the sidebar manageable
Test instructions before relying on them
— start a test conversation in the new project to verify that Claude behaves as expected before using it for real work
Project Templates and Starting Points

Setting up a well-structured project from scratch requires thinking about what instructions and knowledge the project needs. Rather than starting from a blank slate each time, developing a set of reusable project templates for common use cases saves time and produces more consistent setups.
Template: Writing and Editing Project

# Instructions template for writing/editing projects
Role: You are a professional editor for [publication name].

Audience: [Describe the target reader]

Voice and tone:
- [Tone descriptor 1]
- [Tone descriptor 2]

Style rules:
- Sentence length: [Target average]
- Voice: [Active/passive preference]
- Vocabulary: [Complexity level]
- Formatting: [List preferences]

Things to avoid: [List of prohibited phrases or patterns]

Output format:
[Describe what the final output should look like]

Knowledge files to add:
- Style guide document
- Examples of good published pieces
- List of preferred/avoided terms
Template: Technical Documentation Project

# Instructions template for technical documentation
Role: Technical writer for [product name]

Technology: [Tech stack, version]

Documentation standards:
- Follow [style guide name, e.g., Google Developer Style Guide]
- Use present tense in descriptions
- Use imperative mood in procedures ("Click Save")
- Include code examples for all API calls

Audience: [Developer experience level and context]

Output structure for how-to guides:
1. Prerequisites
2. Steps (numbered)
3. Verification step
4. Troubleshooting

Knowledge files to add:
- Product feature list
- API reference
- Screenshot library or link
Template: Code Review Project

# Instructions template for code review projects
Role: Senior engineer for [language/framework] codebase

Tech stack: [List technologies]

Review priority order:
1. Security (report all findings)
2. Correctness
3. Performance (only if obvious)
4. Style and conventions

Coding standards:
- [Language-specific conventions]
- [Testing requirements]
- [Documentation requirements]

Format for findings:
- One finding per bullet point
- Include: severity, file/line if known, issue, recommendation

Knowledge files to add:
- Coding standards document
- Security checklist
- Architecture overview
Multi-User Projects and Team Collaboration

On Claude.ai Team and Enterprise plans, projects can be shared across workspace members. A shared project gives everyone on the team access to the same instructions and knowledge base, ensuring consistent AI behavior without each member needing to maintain their own setup.
Setting Up Shared Projects
Create the project from the account with admin or appropriate permissions.
Add instructions and knowledge files before sharing so team members start with a complete setup.
Invite team members through the project's sharing settings.
Set appropriate permission levels (view only, can add conversations, can edit instructions).
Document the project's purpose and any conventions in the project description or a pinned conversation.
Managing Shared Project Quality

Shared projects introduce coordination overhead: multiple team members may want to change instructions, add knowledge files, or rename the project. Establish ownership clearly — designate one or two team members responsible for maintaining instructions and knowledge quality. Others can add conversations but should not modify shared settings unilaterally.

Track knowledge file versions by including the date or version in file names: 'style-guide-v2-2025-06.md'. When updating a shared knowledge file, notify team members so they understand the change and can test the updated behavior.
Project Lifecycle Management
The Active Phase

During the active phase, a project is in regular use. Instructions and knowledge should be refined based on actual usage: if a certain type of output keeps needing correction, update the instructions to address it. If a frequently referenced document changes, update the knowledge file. Active maintenance during this phase directly improves output quality over time.
The Maintenance Phase

When a project is used intermittently rather than daily, maintenance becomes periodic rather than continuous. Check knowledge files quarterly for staleness and update instructions when the underlying task requirements evolve. An intermittently used project that is never updated gradually degrades in quality as the real-world context drifts from the project's frozen knowledge.
Archiving and Closing Projects

When a project is truly complete — the product it supported has launched, the client engagement has ended, the research has been published — take the time to archive it properly. Export or save any important conversations that contain valuable outputs. Then rename the project with an 'ARCHIVE:' prefix or delete it to keep the active project list clean. A cluttered project sidebar with dozens of old projects makes it harder to navigate to the ones you actually use.
Troubleshooting Projects
Claude Is Not Following Instructions

If Claude in a project conversation is not following the project instructions, check the following: First, verify you are in a project conversation, not a standalone chat — navigate to the project and start a new conversation there, not from the main new chat button. Second, check whether the instructions are saved — look at the project's Instructions tab. Third, try a very direct test: start a conversation and in the first message say 'What are your operating instructions?' Claude should quote or summarize the instructions if it has received them. If it describes default behavior instead, the instructions may not be loading correctly.
Instructions Are Too Long

Extremely long instructions may be truncated in some interface versions or may crowd out knowledge files in the context window. If instructions exceed the character limit shown in the editor, trim them. Prioritize the most impactful constraints and move detailed reference material to knowledge files rather than embedding it in the instructions.
Knowledge Files Are Not Being Used

Claude may have the knowledge file in context but not reference it explicitly. Test by asking a question that can only be answered from the knowledge file — for example, asking about a specific term from your uploaded glossary. If Claude gives a generic answer, explicitly mention the file: 'Based on the API spec in the project knowledge, what does the /users endpoint return?' Adding explicit cross-references in the instructions ('Refer to the api-spec.yaml in project knowledge for all API-related questions') also helps.
Measuring Project Effectiveness
After using a project for a few weeks, assess whether it is actually improving your workflow. Useful metrics to consider:
Setup time saved
— how much time is saved per session by not re-entering context manually?
Revision rounds
— are outputs from this project requiring fewer editing rounds than before the project existed?
Consistency
— are outputs across multiple sessions more consistent in tone, format, and accuracy than they would be without the project context?
Error rate
— are there specific types of errors the project eliminates that occurred before?

If the project is not delivering measurable benefit, the most common causes are: instructions that are too vague to change behavior, knowledge files that don't contain what Claude needs, or a task type that is too varied to benefit from fixed context. In these cases, invest time in improving the instructions and knowledge rather than abandoning the project approach.
Key Takeaways
Projects are persistent workspaces in Claude.ai that store instructions, knowledge files, and related conversations so you don't need to re-establish context each session.
Create a project by clicking New Project in the sidebar, giving it a descriptive name, and then adding instructions and knowledge files before starting conversations.
Good project names are specific, 4–6 words, and describe the function and context of the work (e.g., 'Code Review — Python Backend').
Projects improve consistency and response quality for recurring tasks by giving Claude stable, detailed context from the start of every conversation.
Each conversation in a project starts from the shared instructions and knowledge but is otherwise independent — projects do not share conversational history across chats.
Create projects for genuinely recurring use cases; avoid over-fragmenting your work into too many thin projects that are hard to navigate.
How to Use This Feature Effectively

Getting the most out of Creating Projects requires understanding both its capabilities and its limitations. This section provides concrete guidance on using the feature to its full potential, with practical examples you can apply immediately.

The key to effective use of Creating Projects is to start with clear intentions. Know what outcome you're aiming for before you begin, and set up the feature in a way that supports that outcome. Many users find it helpful to experiment with different approaches to discover what works best for their specific workflow.
Define your goal clearly before you start — vague intentions lead to vague results
Use the feature consistently to build familiarity and identify patterns
Combine with other Claude features for maximum effectiveness
Review outputs critically and provide feedback through follow-up prompts
Document what works for your use case so you can replicate success
Share knowledge with teammates to build organizational competency
Use Cases and Applications

There are many valuable use cases for Creating Projects across different contexts. Understanding the range of applications helps you identify where this feature can add the most value to your workflow.
Professional productivity: Streamline repetitive tasks and focus on higher-value work
Research and analysis: Gather and synthesize information more efficiently
Content creation: Generate first drafts, outlines, and creative ideas
Learning and education: Explore new topics and get explanations of complex concepts
Problem-solving: Work through challenges with an AI thinking partner
Code assistance: Write, review, debug, and explain code
Communication: Draft, edit, and polish written communications
Integration with Your Workflow
Integrating Creating Projects into your existing workflow is usually straightforward, but there are several strategies that can make the transition smoother and ensure you get maximum value.

Start by identifying the specific tasks where you'll use this feature most. Then create a small experiment to test how it fits into your existing process. Adjust your workflow based on what you learn, and gradually expand usage as you build confidence.
Map out your current workflow before adding Claude to identify integration points
Start with one specific task to build confidence before expanding usage
Create templates or prompts that you can reuse across similar tasks
Set up a feedback loop to continuously improve your approach
Track time savings and quality improvements to build the business case
Configuration and Settings

Configuring Creating Projects correctly for your needs is important for getting consistent, high-quality results. While the defaults work well for most use cases, understanding the available options allows you to fine-tune the behavior.

Take time to explore the settings and options available. Each setting serves a specific purpose, and understanding what each one does gives you greater control over the output. Don't be afraid to experiment — you can always revert to defaults if needed.
Review all available settings before assuming defaults are optimal for your use case
Make changes incrementally so you can understand the impact of each adjustment
Save your preferred configurations for easy reuse across sessions
Test configuration changes with representative examples before relying on them in production
Document your configuration choices and the reasons behind them
Tips and Tricks

Experienced users of Creating Projects have discovered many tips and tricks that significantly improve their results. These insights often come from experimentation and aren't always obvious from the documentation.
Use specific, concrete language rather than vague requests for better results
Break complex tasks into smaller steps for more reliable outcomes
Provide context and examples when working on specialized or domain-specific tasks
Use follow-up prompts to refine and improve initial outputs
Combine this feature with other Claude capabilities for compound benefits
Learn from both successes and failures to continuously improve your approach
Share tips with your team to build collective expertise
Detailed Walkthrough

This walkthrough takes you through Creating Projects step by step, explaining not just what to do but why each step matters. Following along will build both your practical skills and your conceptual understanding.

One of the most common challenges people face with Creating Projects is understanding how all the pieces fit together. This walkthrough addresses that by building up from first principles, showing how each component contributes to the overall result.
Step 1: Understand the goal — what specific outcome are you trying to achieve?
Step 2: Gather prerequisites — ensure you have everything needed before starting
Step 3: Configure the basics — set up the minimal working configuration first
Step 4: Test the basics — verify the simple case works before adding complexity
Step 5: Add features incrementally — build up functionality one piece at a time
Step 6: Handle edge cases — ensure robust behavior for unusual inputs
Step 7: Optimize and polish — refine the implementation for production use
Real-World Scenarios
Understanding how Creating Projects applies to real-world scenarios helps bridge the gap between learning and application. These scenarios represent common situations you'll encounter in practice.

Scenario 1: A startup team wants to quickly build a proof-of-concept. They need to move fast, so they prioritize simplicity and speed over optimization. This is the perfect situation to use the minimal configuration and iterate.

Scenario 2: An enterprise with strict compliance requirements needs to implement this feature with full audit logging, access controls, and documentation. They'll spend more time upfront designing the architecture correctly.
Scenario 3: A solo developer building a personal project wants maximum flexibility and customization. They can afford to experiment more freely because the stakes are lower.
Match your approach to your context — startups and enterprises have different constraints
Document your decisions early, even in prototypes, to avoid losing context later
Build in testing and validation from the start, regardless of the project size
Consider future requirements, but don't over-engineer for hypothetical needs
Troubleshooting Common Issues
When working with Creating Projects, you'll likely encounter some common issues. Understanding these in advance saves significant time and frustration.
Issue 1 — Getting unexpected results: This is often caused by incomplete or ambiguous instructions. Review your input carefully, be more specific about what you want, and try providing examples.

Issue 2 — Performance is slower than expected: Check if you're using the right model for your use case, consider whether you can reduce the amount of context you're sending, and look for opportunities to cache results.
Issue 3 — Inconsistent outputs: Add more constraints and examples to your prompts, use structured output formats, and consider breaking complex tasks into simpler subtasks.
Log all inputs and outputs during development to diagnose issues
Compare against known-good examples when outputs seem wrong
Check the Anthropic documentation for recent changes that might affect behavior
Use the Claude.ai interface to test prompts before integrating via the API
Advanced Configuration
Once you've mastered the basics of Creating Projects, you can explore advanced configuration options that unlock additional capabilities.
Custom parameters: Adjust model parameters to fine-tune behavior for your specific use case
System prompts: Use system-level instructions to establish consistent behavior across interactions
Multi-turn conversations: Leverage conversation history to build context over multiple turns
Tool integration: Combine with Claude's tool use capabilities for more powerful applications
Caching strategies: Use prompt caching to reduce costs and latency for repeated requests
Streaming: Implement streaming responses for real-time user experiences
Batch processing: Process multiple requests efficiently using batch APIs
Summary

This section provided a comprehensive exploration of Creating Projects, from basic usage through advanced scenarios. The key takeaways are: start simple and iterate, match your approach to your context, and always test with representative examples. With these foundations in place, you're ready to apply these skills confidently in your own projects.
